segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter Sixteen: The Coalition

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav Sorn's chambers were in the mid-ring, two streets above the Fade Line, in a building that used to be a counting house. Cass had been there once before, delivering a tuning report his father had been too tired to carry. He remembered the door — heavy oak, a brass plate, no name on it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He knocked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Footsteps. The door opened and Dav looked at him with the expression of a man who had been expecting someone else, then recalibrated.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: Bellwright,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need to talk to you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause. Then he stepped back.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The chambers smelled of old paper and the beeswax Dav used on his practice board. A single lamp burned against the gray morning. Sheet music stacked in a way that said he knew exactly where everything was.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stood in the middle of the room and did not sit.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need you to file a formal challenge to my brother's contract.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav said nothing. He moved to the table and stood on the other side of it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Not on the lame chord grounds,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The Civic Pitch. The ratification reference tone.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He kept his voice level, because if the urgency got in now the rest of the conversation would be harder.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It encodes a question of consent that's never been answered. Aldric built a question into the first bells and the system has been running on top of it for two hundred years without anyone answering it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Outside, a cart went past on the stone below.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: That's not a legal argument anyone can make with tally-songs,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: Or sealed records.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: No. You're describing a formal hearing. Academy chambers. Someone standing in that room and demonstrating the sub-harmonic frequency in the bells, live, in front of a registrar.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav looked at him.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: [worried] You're asking me to end my career.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm asking you to use it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The lamp flame moved with a draft from somewhere — a gap in the window frame, probably — then steadied.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The silence had texture, the way silence did in a room with good acoustics. Cass counted seven seconds of it and heard Dav breathing and the faint hum of the street through the floorboards.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Then Dav put two fingers against his throat and this time left them there.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: What's the frequency,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass told him. The pitch number, the interval, the position in the harmonic sequence — where it sat in the Civic Pitch, how far below the audible range it would appear to a normal ear, how someone trained in breath control and resonance might feel it before they heard it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav was quiet for a different reason now.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: I've heard it,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass waited.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: Every Founding Day for twenty years. Just before the noon strike — something in the bass register. I thought it was the acoustics of the square. I thought it was my ear doing something strange in the cold.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: I've heard it every year and never known what it was.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Twenty years. Cass had been alive for fourteen of them. Dav Sorn had been standing in the Civic Square on Founding Day since before Cass was born, hearing the question and not knowing it was a question.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: I'll file the petition,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it as you struck a bell. Clean, and then it was done.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Vella Ashvale's house was on the upper edge of the Academy District, a narrow building with a green door and a practice room on the ground floor whose shutters Cass could hear were closed — the sound from inside muffled but present, someone running scales. He stood outside for a moment. The scales stopped.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He knocked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Vella opened the door herself. She was a compact woman, forty or so, with the upright posture of someone who had spent twenty years managing breath. She looked at him without surprise. She'd been expecting something like this, or something like him, for longer than he'd known.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: Bellwright,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need to talk to you about the petition.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She stepped back and let him in.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The practice room was still warm from whoever had been in it. A music stand in the corner, a chair pushed back at an angle. On the wall: a framed copy of her Court Singer's license, the Academy seal at the bottom, her name in the registrar's formal hand. Vella Ashvale, Licensed Court Singer, Third Tier, Cantamura Academy of Civic Harmony. Cass had seen documents like it before. He'd never stood in front of one while asking someone to make it worthless.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She didn't offer him a seat. She stood by the music stand with her arms at her sides, waiting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He told her what he needed. The ratification witness — her name on the petition, her professional confirmation that the reference tone contained the structural defect, her willingness to stand in the hearing room and sing the interval herself if the panel required a licensed voice. He kept it precise. He did not soften it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: When he finished, Vella was quiet for a moment. She looked at the framed license on the wall, not at him. Then she said,

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: Lenne has been studying for four years.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: She tests for her provisional certification in the spring. If I sign this and the Academy rules against us — and they may rule against us — she doesn't test. They'll find a procedural reason to delay her. A year, maybe two. Maybe permanently, if Suvaine decides to make an example.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass didn't say anything. He'd known this was part of it when he walked to her door. He'd thought about whether to name it first or let her name it, and he'd decided that walking in with a softened version was worse than the thing itself.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And if we win,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Vella looked at him.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: If we win, the petition resets the reference tone question. Every coerced binding in the last twenty years is contestable. Suvaine loses the procedural discretion she's been using to keep Volume Four blank. The Academy has to codify the exceptional circumstances. They have to fill the pages.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Lenne tests for her certification in a city where the system she's entering actually does what it says it does.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A long silence. Outside, a door closed somewhere on the street.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: You're fourteen.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: You're standing in my practice room telling me what my daughter's future is worth.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm telling you what it costs. I don't know what it's worth. That's yours.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She looked at him for another long moment — steady, the way Lenne looked at him, that same quality of attention that didn't perform anything. He understood suddenly where Lenne had learned it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Vella turned to the music stand. A sheet of blank staff paper was already on it, which might have been coincidence and might not. She picked up the pen that was lying across it.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: Tell me what to write,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Mira Fen was in the Academy library when Cass arrived, which was where she spent most mornings that she wasn't teaching. She was sixty-three, or thereabouts — Cass had never been certain, and her face didn't resolve the question. She had the specific unhurried quality of someone who had been right about something for a long time and had given up being angry about it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She looked up when he sat down across from her.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: I've been wondering when you'd come,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He told her what he needed: an academic argument establishing the structural defect in the reference tone, grounded in tonal theory, in a form the Academy's review panel couldn't dismiss as outer-district agitation or a child's conjecture. Something with citations. Something with her name on it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Mira listened. When he finished, she was quiet for a moment. She looked at the books open in front of her — not at him.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: I wrote it once before,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: Thirty-one years ago. I was thirty-two, junior faculty, and I'd been hearing the structural gap in the reference tone for two years. I wrote the argument. Twelve pages.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She turned one of the open books toward him — not to show him anything in it, just a gesture, something to do with her hands.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: On page eleven, a colleague read it over my shoulder. He said it was interesting. He said it was destabilizing. He said the Academy had enough to manage without reopening the foundational question of whether every contract ratified in the last two centuries was contingent on an unanswered question.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What did you do?

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: I burned it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She said it without drama.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: Not the whole thing. I got to page eleven and I stopped. I burned what I'd written and I went back to teaching the system as written.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She paused.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: I have taught the system as written for thirty-one years. I know exactly what it contains and exactly what it doesn't. I have been composing around the same gap in my lectures that your brother has been composing around in his contract music, and we have never met, and I imagine we would understand each other very well.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said nothing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] He looked at her hands on the table. They were resting beside the open book, fingers slightly curled, the ink stain on the outer edge of her right palm worn into the skin like a permanent shadow. Thirty-one years of holding a chalk and writing the system as written. The same hand that had held the pages while they burned.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He tried to imagine it — being thirty-two and knowing, and then being thirty-three and teaching anyway. And then forty. And then fifty. Walking into the lecture hall every autumn with the new students sitting in rows, their faces clean and ready, and opening the Codex to the tonal reference section and saying

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] this is how it works

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: while knowing, in the exact place where pitch lived in your body, that it wasn't. Not entirely. That there was a gap the size of a quarter-tone running through the whole system, and you had seen it, and you had written it down, and you had burned what you'd written and gone back to your desk.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Every lecture. Every student. Every year. The quiet act of handing someone an incomplete instrument and watching them build a career on it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Mira was watching him think. She didn't rush it. She had the patience of someone who had waited thirty-one years for the right person to sit down across from her and ask the right question, and now that it was happening, she could wait another minute.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [softly] I would have done the same thing,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said. He wasn't sure it was true. He said it because she needed to hear it from someone who was about to ask her to undo it.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: No, you wouldn't have,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said. Not unkindly.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: That's why you're here.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: Give me twenty minutes,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Mira said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She wrote it in the Academy library, at a table in the east alcove where the morning light came in flat and cold off the limestone wall. Cass sat across from her and did not interrupt. He watched her hands — not to be unkind, but because he couldn't help it, as you couldn't help watching a bell be cast. Her hands were a teacher's hands, ink-stained at the index finger and the outer edge of the palm, the joints enlarged from decades of gripping chalk and pen. They moved across the page without hesitation. She wasn't reconstructing the argument from memory. She was writing the thing she'd been not-writing for thirty-one years, and it came out in twenty minutes because it had been ready for thirty-one years, waiting behind her teeth.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: When she finished, she set down the pen. She looked at the pages — twelve of them, the same length as the argument she'd burned, which Cass suspected was not an accident. Then she picked them up and held them out across the table.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Her hands, doing that, were completely steady.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: Don't lose this,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: I'm not writing it a third time.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The meeting room above Torvald's dye-works smelled of sulfur and indigo — the fermentation tanks running along the south wall of the building below had worked themselves into every surface, the scarred wood of the table, the plaster, the air itself. Six of them around the table: Torvald at the head, Dav on his left, Vella Ashvale beside Dav. Lenne had come with her mother, which no one had planned and no one objected to. Mira Fen had arrived before any of them and was already in the chair at the far end, her bag at her feet, two books open in front of her.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stood where Torvald gestured him to stand.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He laid out the shape of it: Dav's petition, Vella's ratification, Mira's academic argument establishing the structural defect in the reference tone, Torvald's tally-songs as a pattern of evidence across two decades of outer-district disputes. The Academy would be required to convene a formal hearing. At the hearing, Cass would demonstrate the sub-harmonic live, using the Bell Tower's acoustic connection to the Civic Square.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room was quiet, but it was not the quiet of people waiting for the rest. It was the quiet of people doing arithmetic — counting what they'd agreed to, holding the shape of it in their heads, checking it against the shape of what they stood to lose.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Vella was looking at the table. She'd been steady at her music stand that morning, pen in hand, writing what he'd asked. Here, in this room, with Dav beside her and Mira at the far end and Torvald's big stained hands flat on the wood, the steadiness had a different quality. She'd said yes in private. Saying it in company was a different interval.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: I want to be clear about something,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said. She didn't raise her voice.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: When I sign the ratification witness, I'm not just lending my name to a petition. I'm the only independent Court Singer in this city whose name means anything to the Academy panel. Dav's name means something too

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: — she glanced at him —

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: but they expect Dav to push. They don't expect me.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: No,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: So when I sign it, I'm the thing they didn't see coming. Which means I'm also the thing they'll come for first.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She looked at him steadily.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: I'm not asking you to talk me out of it. I'm asking you to tell me you understand that.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I understand.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: Lenne is in this room.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I'm in this room because I chose to be.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Vella looked at her daughter for a moment — something passed between them that Cass couldn't fully read, some negotiation that had been running since long before today. Then Vella set her hands flat on the table, the same gesture Torvald had been holding since the start.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: All right,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: I understand the shape of it.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: [tense] There's a problem,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at him.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: The demonstration — if I'm following your reasoning, and I may not be — only works during a live Civic Pitch. The sub-harmonic sits inside the sequence. You can't — no. Let me say it correctly. You can't separate it and have it mean anything acoustically. Not to a panel.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav's voice was even, composing.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: In my assessment, you need an emergency pitch while you're standing in the hearing room. You need a reason that would require it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Maret has filed to take the bells,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it without preamble, the way he said most things — Cass had noticed that about him, the way he moved through the edges of a conversation and went straight to the weight of it. He leaned forward slightly.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: It's been sitting in the Academy's review queue for six weeks — six weeks, mind you, gathering dust like bolt-ends in the back of a warehouse, and nobody's touched it. The Bellwright family hasn't contested it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Now here's where it gets interesting, because the custodianship rules are the one thread in this whole cloth that actually runs straight — if the Bellwright family contests the transfer, the Academy has to convene a formal review. Not

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: *Has to*. Bell custodianship requires the bells to be assessed in operation. Which means a live Civic Pitch. Which means all parties present, in one room, with the bells actually ringing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass had known about the transfer filing. He'd found it in the queue records three weeks ago, a single line in the Academy's administrative register, and he'd stood in the corridor outside the records room for a long time afterward. He'd thought his father didn't know. He was less sure of that now.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald had said yes before Cass finished explaining the plan, back when Cass first came to him — before Dav, before Vella, before Mira. He'd said yes as you said *obviously*, and then he'd sat back and let Cass keep talking. Now he had his hands on the table, blue-green and still, and he was watching Cass with the patience of a man who had spent twenty years building something and understood that the last piece was always the hardest one.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at him. Torvald's eyes had the quality of someone who had already done the arithmetic and was waiting for Cass to finish doing his. Not impatient. Not pushing. Just present, the way the dye in his hands was present — worked in, permanent, the evidence of a long commitment to a kind of labor.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He'd said yes immediately because this was what the twenty years had been for. Not just the Compact's tally-songs and the community witnesses and the parallel infrastructure he'd built dispute by dispute out of folk intervals the Academy wouldn't recognize. All of it had been waiting for a thread that reached inside the system, and Cass was the thread, and Torvald had understood that before Cass had finished his first sentence.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Your father would have to file the objection,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Mira Fen picked up her pen and set it back down. Lenne was looking at him from across the table — had been looking for the last few minutes, steady, not performing anything.

segments.speaker: VELLA
segments.text: How long do we have.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Dav files tomorrow,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The Academy has seven days to acknowledge the petition. After that —

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: Suvaine will push the scheduling,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav said.

segments.speaker: DAV_SORN
segments.text: She'll find procedural reasons. She can push it thirty days before it becomes a refusal to convene, which is its own violation. Mira's argument means she won't refuse. She'll convene on her own timetable.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Thirty days, worst case,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: MIRA_FEN
segments.text: [softly] He already knows he has to talk to his father. He's been sitting with it since he walked in.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Nobody argued with her.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Can I ask something.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: It wasn't a question. The room turned toward her.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The sub-harmonic,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: When you sing it into audible range. Everyone in the hearing room hears it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: That's the point,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Everyone in the hearing room hears a question they didn't know existed. A question about whether they consent to be bound.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She set her hands flat on the table — the same gesture her mother had made, the same gesture Torvald had been holding since the start.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: They didn't ask to hear it. They didn't choose to walk into a room where someone would put that question in their bodies.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room was very still.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [firmly] How is what you're doing different from what Aldric did?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Her voice was level, her composing-face gone, something underneath it now that was harder and more honest.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: He put a question in people's bodies without their consent. You're about to do the same thing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass opened his mouth.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: He decided the city wasn't ready to choose. So he hid the question below hearing and let the system run on top of it. You've decided the city *is* ready. So you're going to force the question into audible range and let it land wherever it lands.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She looked at him steadily.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The city still doesn't get to decide whether to be asked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The silence had a different quality now. Torvald's hands were still on the table. Dav had gone to his throat and stayed there. Mira Fen had set her pen down.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It's not the same.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Why not.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He tried to find it — the clean distinction, the thing that made what he was doing different from what Aldric had done. Aldric had hidden the question out of fear. Cass was revealing it out of — what? Justice? Urgency? The desperate arithmetic of his brother's contract?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Because they should have been asked from the beginning,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The question was always theirs. I'm giving it back.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: You're deciding *when* they get it back. You're deciding the room, the moment, the conditions.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She didn't look away.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: That's still you choosing for them. You just trust your reasons more than Aldric trusted his.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass had no answer for that. He looked at the table and had no answer.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [softly] She's not wrong.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room stayed quiet. Not the arithmetic-quiet from before — something different now, the silence of people who have agreed to something and are letting it become real. Cass looked at the table. Old wood, scarred from years of use. Someone had carved a small set of interval marks into the corner — folk notation, three notches and a slash, the Compact's shorthand for a contested claim. He put his thumb over it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] They needed Cass to walk into the workshop tonight and ask his father to undo what his father had chosen ten years ago — to stand up and say the bells were not worth what he paid for them, in front of the Academy, in front of Maret Corda, in front of the whole machinery of the system he'd kept running at the cost of his older son's life.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: They needed Eddan Bellwright to say, in a legally binding room, that he had been wrong.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass lifted his thumb from the carved marks.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'll go now,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Before it gets late.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Cass,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The sub-harmonic. When you demonstrate it in the hearing room — can you actually make them hear it? The registrar, the panel. Can you make them hear what you hear?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He'd been thinking about this since the plan first took shape. The honest answer was that he didn't know. He'd never tried to make anyone else hear the thing beneath the Civic Pitch, the question that had been sitting in the bass register for two hundred years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: But Founding Day. Every year. The moment before noon when Dav Sorn had felt something in the Civic Square for twenty years without knowing what it was.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I think so,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: With the bells actually ringing and the right conditions. The frequency is there. It's always been there. I just need to make it loud enough that they can't call it atmospheric interference.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne held his gaze for another moment. Then:

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Okay.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was almost at the door when he looked back.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Lenne had turned toward the shuttered window, and her face had come undone. Not crying — something before crying, or instead of it. The expression of someone calculating a loss they hadn't yet been asked to confirm. Her mother's license on the wall of the practice room. Her own provisional examination in the spring. Four years of study, four years of intervals committed to memory, and all of it balanced on what happened in a hearing room she wouldn't be allowed to enter.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She caught him looking and the composing-face came back — instant, practiced. But for a moment she had been a girl whose future was narrowing, and she knew precisely how narrow it could get.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He went.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The cold outside was sharp after the dye-works warmth. He walked without hurrying, back through the Weavers' District toward the Bell Quarter, past the storage sheds and the low stone wall and the dye-vat smell that thinned as he moved inward toward the city's center. His hands were steady at his sides.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had one more thing to do tonight, before the workshop.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had promised himself he'd write to Perin.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Not about the plan — not yet, not until there was something to tell that couldn't be taken back. But Perin had been sending letters for ten years with specific technical observations buried in them, intervals described in precise terms, weather reports and chamber acoustics and what he'd had for lunch, and Cass had been reading the gaps for months now and he was almost certain he knew what Perin had been trying to say.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He wanted Perin to know someone had heard it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped at the corner by the bakery — closed now, the smell of the day's bread still faint in the cold air — and wrote the letter in his head first, the way Perin had taught him to tune a bell: find the note before you strike it. Know where you're going before the sound begins.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I heard the pattern in your letters. The intervals you describe. I think I know what you've been composing around for twelve years. I think you've been leaving a record in the Corda contract music, in places you knew someone would eventually look, if they knew where to look.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I'm looking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] Hold on a little longer. The petition files tomorrow.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't write *we have a plan* because that felt like the kind of thing that could go wrong between the writing and the reading. He didn't write *I'm afraid* because Perin already knew — it was the frequency underneath everything Cass had ever said to him, the under-note in every letter he'd sent in the last fourteen years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] He just wrote: I heard you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He'd copy it clean when he got home, before the workshop. Then his father.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Bell Quarter was ahead of him, the tower visible above the roofline, the orange warmth of the workshop windows lit against the evening. His father was in there. The lathe was probably running — it usually was, this time of day, the low mechanical whir that meant Eddan had found something to do with his hands and was doing it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass walked toward it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The workshop door was unlocked. He let himself in, and the smell caught him as it always did — linseed oil, metal-dust, the char-trace in the stonework that no amount of cleaning would ever lift. His father was at the lathe. Not turning anything. Just standing with his hands on the frame, the way he stood when the work was done but he wasn't ready to leave.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eddan looked up. Looked at his son. Neither of them spoke.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass hung his coat on the hook by the door and went to the bench. The secondary bells were waiting — the Eleventh of Cooper Lane needed its clapper pin re-seated, a job Cass could do in his sleep. He pulled the pin-setter from the rack and sat down.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: They worked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The quiet in the workshop had been the same his whole life — the held quality of two people who knew how to be in a room together without filling it. But something had shifted since the journals. Since his father's hands had shaken in the kitchen and the oil jar had sat between them on the table. The silence was the same shape, but the thing inside it was different. Before, it had held secrets. Now they both knew what those secrets were, and the silence held something else — the weight of knowing, shared but unspoken, carried between them across the workbench like a tool passed from hand to hand.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The Eleventh needs a new pin, not the old one re-seated.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass set down the pin-setter and picked up the blank stock.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I was going to re-seat it first to check the bore.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Good.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father's hands were steady on the lathe frame. In the workshop they always were. Cass filed the pin blank and listened to the sound of it — the rasp of steel on bronze, clean and specific — and his father stood four feet away with his hands on the empty lathe, and neither of them said what needed saying, and for one evening that was enough.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He finished the pin. Set it in the bore. Tapped it home. The Eleventh rang a clean note when he tested the clapper — not the full voice of the bell, just the small domestic sound of a bell doing what it was built for.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: That'll hold,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass cleaned the tools and hung them. His father was still standing at the lathe. The lamplight made the bronze on the frames glow warm. Outside, the Bell Quarter was quiet, the night settling in.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Goodnight,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Goodnight.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] He went upstairs. He lay in bed and listened to his father not leaving the workshop, the silence of a man standing in the one room where his hands didn't shake. The letter to Perin sat on his desk, uncopied. Tomorrow he'd send it. Tomorrow night he'd come back to this workshop and ask his father the thing he hadn't asked tonight.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] But tonight they'd been in the same room. That was the first step, and it was enough.
